## How It's Calculated

This calculator applies the Carnot efficiency formula with your input values, verifying typical ranges and alerting you to values outside the reasonable range.

## Final Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a specialized professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: Why is Carnot efficiency called a theoretical maximum?
faq.a: Because no real heat engine can exceed the Carnot limit. It represents the absolute thermodynamic upper bound for any cycle operating between two temperature reservoirs.

faq.q: How does Carnot efficiency compare to real car engines?
faq.a: A real gasoline engine achieves about 25-30% efficiency. A Carnot engine with hot reservoir at 2500K would reach ~60% efficiency, but real engines lose enormous amounts of energy to friction, heat loss, and incomplete combustion.

faq.q: What is the Carnot efficiency formula?
faq.a: η = 1 - (T_cold / T_hot), where both temperatures are in absolute units (Kelvin). Temperatures must be greater than 0K, with T_hot always higher than T_cold.

faq.q: How does Carnot efficiency apply to refrigerators?
faq.a: For refrigerators, the measure is called Coefficient of Performance (COP), which is the inverse relationship: COP = T_cold / (T_hot - T_cold). Higher COP means better cooling efficiency.

faq.q: What is the Carnot efficiency of a nuclear power plant?
faq.a: Nuclear plants typically operate with a hot side around 600K. Their real efficiency is about 33%, which is close to the Carnot theoretical limit for those temperatures—unlike coal plants, they're nearly optimal.

faq.q: How can I improve Carnot cycle efficiency?
faq.a: Increase the hot reservoir temperature or decrease the cold reservoir temperature. This is why power plants use superheated steam and cooling towers to maximize the temperature difference.
